summary: Continued replacing hardcoded absolute function-pointer values in asm/data.s with symbolic function labels, and added missing .global func_XXXXXXXX labels at the corresponding addresses across many fighter assembly files so those data-table references could resolve symbolically.
what_changed: In asm/data.s, many .4byte entries that were raw absolute addresses such as 0x80110094, 0x801147C0, and 0x8011B51C were changed to symbolic labels such as func_80110094, func_801147C0, and func_8011B51C.
The PR preserved existing NULL table entries while converting neighboring function-pointer slots to labels.
Several data tables under labels including lbl_803C11D8, lbl_803C13E8, lbl_803C146C, lbl_803C14F0, lbl_803C1EBC, lbl_803C2150, lbl_803C2258, and lbl_803C2800 had pointer entries updated.
A comment near lbl_803C2150 was changed from an uncertain TODO-style note to '# Multi-jump function table', giving the table a more concrete classification.

Fighter assembly files received missing .global func_XXXXXXXX declarations immediately before code at those addresses, enabling asm/data.s to reference those routines by symbol instead of by numeric address.

Examples of newly exported labels include func_80148C64 in asm/ftclink.s, func_8010E1C4 and func_8010FBFC in asm/ftdonkey_2.s, func_800E32B0, func_800E40D8, and func_800E4CF4 in asm/ftfalcon.s, and func_800E72C4, func_800E85EC, and func_800E9EE8 in asm/ftfox.s.
The changed files list shows 24 assembly files touched, with asm/data.s accounting for an equal 170 added and 170 deleted lines, consistent with pointer text replacement rather than layout changes.
smart_moves: Converted data-section function pointers to relocatable symbolic references instead of leaving them as opaque constants, improving cross-reference discoverability and reducing risk when functions are later renamed or moved into C.
Added the required .global labels at exact function entry addresses before updating table references, avoiding unresolved-symbol issues.
Performed the cleanup broadly across fighter-specific assembly files in one pass, which made the function tables in asm/data.s more uniformly symbolic.
Kept NULL values and non-function constants as literals, indicating the update targeted known function pointers rather than blindly rewriting every .4byte value.
Used address-derived func_XXXXXXXX names for still-unknown functions, matching the existing decomp convention while making the pointers searchable.
decomp_lessons: When a .data table contains code addresses, prefer .4byte func_XXXXXXXX over raw .4byte 0xXXXXXXXX once the target address has a label.
Before replacing a numeric pointer in data with a symbol, ensure the target function entry has a visible label and .global declaration in the owning asm file.
Raw address-to-symbol replacement is a useful low-risk decomp step because it improves the project’s symbol graph without requiring semantic function names yet.
Function-pointer tables often span many fighter modules; cleaning them up requires coordinated symbol additions across the relevant asm/ft*.s files.
Do not convert NULL table slots or obvious non-pointer constants just because they appear in the same table; preserve the data structure shape exactly.
Even a small comment update, such as identifying lbl_803C2150 as a multi-jump function table, can help future decompilers recognize the table’s purpose.
naming_conventions: Unknown functions are represented as func_XXXXXXXX, where XXXXXXXX is the uppercase hexadecimal address, e.g. func_80110094 and func_800E32B0.
Data labels retain lbl_XXXXXXXX names, e.g. lbl_803C11D8, lbl_803C13E8, and lbl_803C2150.
Assembly exports use the pattern '.global func_XXXXXXXX' followed immediately by 'func_XXXXXXXX:' at the function entry point.
NULL is kept as the symbolic null table value instead of being rewritten to a numeric zero.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replace hardcoded function pointer words in .data with symbol operands once the target code labels exist.
Introduce labels at exact instruction addresses that already begin a function, typically just before the prologue instruction such as mflr r0.
Use the existing disassembly address comments to verify that the new func_XXXXXXXX label matches the numeric pointer being replaced.
Keep table alignment directives such as .balign 4 and table ordering unchanged to avoid binary layout drift.
For function tables with repeated targets, replace each occurrence of the same address with the same symbol, as seen with repeated entries such as func_80122988 and func_800CD390.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or review records were present in the provided PR context.
Because there was no review text, no reviewer rationale or requested follow-up can be inferred from this slice.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that convert these func_XXXXXXXX symbols to semantic names.
Search asm/data.s for remaining '.4byte 0x80' entries that may still be hardcoded function pointers.
Audit function tables around lbl_803C11D8, lbl_803C13E8, lbl_803C146C, lbl_803C14F0, and lbl_803C2800 for possible struct definitions.
Check whether the '# Multi-jump function table' near lbl_803C2150 was later renamed or moved into C data.
Look for remaining fighter asm files with function entry points lacking .global labels.
